text:
Andy Murrayrevealed that he was almost forced to miss his emotional Olympics send-off as doctors told him he wouldn't recover in time to compete in the Paris 2024 Games after undergoing spinal cyst surgery in late June.

The former world No. 1 officially entered retirement after he and Dan Evanslost their men's doubles quarter-finalto Taylor Fritz and Tommy Paul at the Olympics.

Murray and Evans had saved multiple match points to make it to the last eight in the men's doubles draw. And the two-time gold medallist confessed that he was in pain after ending his spectacular career.

"It's been really hard. Physically, pain-wise, I feel bad," Murray said, addressing a huddle of reporters in the middle of a thunderstorm. "Physically I can obviously go on the court and perform at a level that's competitive. We were close to getting in the medal rounds here.

"That's okay but the pain and discomfort in my body is not good and that's also why I'm happy to be finishing. Because if I kept going and kept trying, eventually you end up having an injury potentially ending your career. So I know that now is the right time and physically, it's been really hard."

Murray was in tears as he waved goodbye to the crowd one last time. But he admitted that he was happy to end his career now after doctors told him that he wouldn't be able to play inWimbledonor Paris 2024. He continued: "I knew that moment was coming for the last few months. And yeah, if it didn’t happen today that was going to be in a couple of days time and I was ready for it and obviously it was emotional because it’s the last time I will play a competitive match.

"But I am genuinely, I am happy just now. Like, I’m happy with how it finished. I’m glad I got to go out here at the Olympics and finish on my terms because at times in the last few years that wasn’t a certainty.

Just in:Tennis star nearly collapsed with heat stroke after Swiatek row at Olympics

"And even a few months ago, I was told when I first went to have my scan on my back, the issue that I had with it I was told that I wouldn’t be playing at the Olympics and I wouldn’t be playing at Wimbledon. So I feel also lucky that I got that opportunity to play here and have some great matches, create amazing memories and yeah, I feel good."

Ahead of the grass season, Murray confessed that he was dreading retirement. But his perspective changed before his fitting send-off at the Olympics and he admitted his injury scare was the turning point. The three-time Grand Slam champion added: "Well, the surgery on my back [changed my perspective], really.

"When I wasn't able to walk properly, and my leg wasn't functioning properly, and I was told, if the cyst continues to grow and puts more pressure on the nerves, it turns into like an emergency situation where you can't control your bladder and you'll lose control of the other leg and it's like, 'Okay, well, that's kind of when I knew it was time'. Thankfully the surgeon did a brilliant job and obviously the rehab process got fast-tracked to try and play at Wimbledon and obviously here. But when that happened I was looking forward to the end after that, because I knew I wanted to finish this summer.

Back in February, the former world No. 1 first announced that he would likely stop playing in summer. But it wasn't until recently that he completely committed to his ending. "That was what I felt like I wanted but there was still some uncertainty there," he said.

"Because I was like, 'Well, what if I do really well over the grass, or what if you know my level improves?' I was starting to play well in Miami, for example, but once I had the meeting with the doctor and understood what the problem was, I was like 'Okay, well, let's try and see if we can get through Wimbledon and prepare well for the Olympics and try and have a good run here.'"

They did have a good run, saving five consecutive match points in round one and two more in their second match. Murray even saved one against Fritz and Paul to get a crucial break on Thursday night before they eventually lost 6-2 6-4. And it sums up the legacy he wants to leave behind.

"I think the last three matches have been a good kind of example of that really. Even today, although we played a poor match, we made it really exciting at the end, we fought right to the death, we came back from, I don't know how many match points we saved, but it was a lot over the course of three matches," the 37-year-old explained.

"We just kept going and I think that's probably, aside from the technicalities of how I hit the ball and moved around the court, my personality, there's things that I did on the court that I certainly wish I wasn't like, I wish I'd behaved differently at times on the court, but there's lots of things about my character on the court that I did like. I love that I always fought and gave it a go right until the end of all my matches. I had a really strong work ethic."

title: Andy Murray almost missed Olympics on doctors' orders as 'really hard' journey shared | Tennis | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Yasmin Syed
description: Andy Murray ended his career in the quarter-final of the Olympics.
